1: The census narrative’s opening differs in one critical word from its Samuel parallel: where 2 Sam 24:1 reads wayyōsep ʾap YHWH laḥărōt bᵉyiśrāʾēl wayyāset ʾet dāwîḏ bāhem, ‘again the anger of YHWH was kindled against Israel, and he incited David against them,’ the Chronicler writes wayyaʿamod śāṭān ʿal yiśrāʾēl wayyāset ʾet dāwîḏ limpōr ʾet yiśrāʾēl, ‘and Satan stood up against Israel and incited David to number Israel.’ The Chronicles version substitutes śāṭān for the divine agency of Samuel. The word śāṭān appears here without the definite article (unlike Job 1-2 and Zech 3:1-2 where it is haśśāṭān, ‘the adversary’) — some read it as a proper name, others as the noun ‘an adversary.’ The substitution reflects a theological development in the post-exilic period: the distinction between divine sovereignty (YHWH ultimately permits) and proximate agency (a hostile adversarial being incites) that the book of Job articulates and that becomes more developed in 2nd Temple Judaism. The Chronicler does not contradict Samuel: YHWH remains sovereign (the plague is YHWH’s judgment; the staying of the plague is YHWH’s mercy, 21:15). The Chronicler disaggregates the causation that Samuel telescopes. The trajectory from haśśāṭān in Zechariah (3:1-2, contemporary with the Chronicler’s era) to the NT’s diabolos and satanas passes through this terminological shift.
22: David’s request to purchase the threshing floor of Ornan the Jebusite — tᵉnāh lî mᵉqôm haggōren, ‘give me the place of the threshing floor’ — establishes the site that 2 Chr 3:1 will identify as Mount Moriah: wayyāḥel šᵉlōmōh liḇnôt ʾet bêt YHWH bîrûšālaim bᵉhar hammōriyyāh, ‘Solomon began to build the house of YHWH in Jerusalem on Mount Moriah.’ The Chronicler makes explicit what the Samuel account leaves implicit: the threshing floor purchased under plague-judgment is the same mountain as the Moriah of Gen 22:2, where YHWH commanded Abraham, lᵉḵ lᵉḵā ʾel ʾereṣ hammōriyyāh wᵉhaʿălēhû šām lᵉʿōlāh, ‘go to the land of Moriah, and offer him there as a burnt offering.’ The convergence is theologically loaded: the site where YHWH stayed Abraham’s knife and provided the substitute sacrifice (Gen 22:13-14), and where YHWH’s destroying angel was stayed (1 Chr 21:15-16), becomes the site of the permanent temple where sacrifice is offered in perpetuity. The ram caught in the thicket (Gen 22), the plague-stayed angel over the threshing floor (1 Chr 21), and the daily burnt offerings on the temple altar (2 Chr 2:4) form a single sacrificial sequence. The name YHWH-yireh (‘YHWH will provide’, Gen 22:14) is the theology of the site — a provision that the NT reads as anticipating the ultimate sacrifice (John 1:29; Heb 10:5-10).
2: śᵉʾû ("count, muster") is the census verb; mibᵉʾēr šebaʿ ʿad-dān ("from Beersheba to Dan") reverses the conventional south-to-north idiom — the Chronicler writes from a Jerusalem-centered perspective rather than the northern vantage of the Deuteronomistic History. Joab and the "army commanders" (śārê hāʿām) are the census agents; the military framing reveals David's purpose — this is a war-preparation census rather than a Mosaic cultic numbering.
3: Joab's objection deploys a formal wish-formula: yôsep YHWH ʿal-ʿammô meʾâ pᵉʿāmîm ("may YHWH add to his people a hundredfold") — prayer and protest simultaneously. The rhetorical question ʾăḏōnî hammelek ʾăḥîhem ("are they not all, my lord the king, his servants?") frames the census as a category error: YHWH's people cannot be numbered as a military asset. The verb ʾāšām (incur guilt) at the end carries full legal weight — Joab predicts cultic violation, not just political miscalculation.
4: wayyiḥzaq dᵉḇar-hammelek ʿal-yôʾāḇ — "the king's word prevailed over Joab" — the verb ḥzq (be strong, overpower) signals inappropriate coercion. David's royal command overrides prophetic-priestly caution; this is the Chronicler's structural explanation for why discipline followed: leadership failed to heed legitimate counsel. wayyēleḵ yôʾāḇ wayᵉhî bᵉḵol-yiśrāʾēl wayyāšāḇ yᵉrûšālim — the census circuit encompassed all Israel before reporting back, suggesting weeks of travel.
5: The numbers differ significantly from 2 Sam 24:9 (800,000 Israel / 500,000 Judah vs. 1,100,000 / 470,000 here). Chronicles' higher Israel figure may reflect a different counting methodology (including military reserves or all eligible males), or may preserve a variant source. The Chronicler does not harmonize the discrepancy; both texts stand in the canon as complementary witnesses. šōlēp ḥereḇ ("able to wield a sword") confirms the military census framing — this is warrior-counting, not Mosaic covenant numbering.
6: Levi and Benjamin's exclusion from the census is the Chronicler's addition to the Samuel parallel. wᵉlēwî wᵉḇinyāmîn lōʾ pāqad bᵉtôḵām — Levi was never numbered in Mosaic censuses (Num 1:49; 2:33); Benjamin's exclusion may reflect the fact that the future temple site at Ornan's threshing floor lay within Benjamin's tribal territory (Josh 18:28), making Benjamin's people the custodians of sacred space rather than military conscripts. Joab's act of mercy toward these two tribes foreshadows the temple-centered restoration of both.
7: wayyēraʿ bᵉʿênê hāʾĕlōhîm — "it was evil/displeasing in the eyes of God." The narrator's use of generic hāʾĕlōhîm here rather than the covenant name YHWH may mark this as a straightforward moral evaluation before the personal covenant dimension emerges in God's disciplinary response. wayyak ʾet-yiśrāʾēl — "he struck Israel" — the collective punishment falls on the people David numbered, not only on David. Covenantal solidarity means the nation bears the consequence of its leader's hubris.
8: David's confession: ḥāṭāʾtî mᵉʾōd ("I have sinned greatly") uses the intensifying adverb mᵉʾōd to mark the gravity of the admission. The petition haʿăḇēr nāʾ ʾet-ʿăwōn ʿaḇdᵉḵā uses ʿāḇar ("cause to pass over") for forgiveness — a spatial metaphor for removing guilt from one's account. kiskalatî mᵉʾōd — "I have acted very foolishly" — sāḵal refers to foolishness that causes harm, the wisdom-tradition antonym of understanding. David recognizes his failure as both moral sin and practical folly.
10: YHWH's three-choice oracle delivered through Gad (ḥōzeh dāwîd — "David's seer") exemplifies the prophetic intermediary role in Chronicles: YHWH communicates discipline options rather than simply imposing punishment unilaterally. The phrase šālaš ʾānī nōṭeh ʿāleḵā ("I am stretching/extending three things toward you") — nāṭâ describes both extending a hand in offer and extending a hand in punishment, creating a deliberate ambiguity: choosing is itself an act within God's sovereign frame.
11: Gad's enumeration begins with bāḥar-lᵉḵā ʾaḥat mēhem — "choose one of them for yourself" — the language of personal selection. The three options — famine, military defeat, plague — correspond precisely to the covenant curse triad of Deut 28 and Lev 26 (sword, famine, pestilence), though reversed in order. The census sin against the covenant people is answered by covenant-curse options; discipline fits the offense structurally.
12: šāloš šānîm rāʿāb (three years famine) / šᵉlōšâ ḥŏdāšîm (three months before enemies' sword) / šᵉlōšet yāmîm ḥereḇ YHWH wᵉdeḇer (three days YHWH's sword and plague) — the three options scale inversely: more human control (famine, long but survivable) → less human control (war, determined by enemies) → no human control (divine plague, the briefest but most total). The interplay of timescales creates a theology of divine initiative: YHWH's direct hand moves fastest and most decisively.
13: David's response is the theological heart of the chapter: naple nāʾ bᵉyad-YHWH kî-raḇbîm raḥămāyw — "let me fall into YHWH's hand, for his mercies are great." raḥămîm (mercies, plural of intensity) is the compassion-word rooted in reḥem (womb) — YHWH's mercies are maternal, tender, and abundant. The opposite refusal — ûḇᵉyad-ʾādām ʾal-ʾeppōl ("but let me not fall into human hands") — reflects the wisdom that human enemies show less mercy than the God whose character is defined by covenant faithfulness (ḥesed and raḥămîm).
14: wayyittēn YHWH deḇer bᵉyiśrāʾēl — the plague falls immediately and directly. šiḇʿîm ʾelep ʾîš (seventy thousand men) — precisely those counted in the census die, an ironic fulfillment of Joab's warning. The act of counting them for military purposes ends with those counted removed from Israel's military potential. The Chronicler's narrative insists that national strength flows from covenant fidelity, not demographic calculation.
15: The theophany at Jerusalem is one of Chronicles' most dramatic scenes: wayyišlaḥ hāʾĕlōhîm malʾāḵ lîrûšālim lᵉšaḥătāh — God sends an angel to destroy Jerusalem. Then the pivot: wayyinnāḥem YHWH ʿal-hārāʿâ — YHWH relented/repented of the disaster; nāḥam is the strongest anthropomorphic term for divine change of emotional state. The command rab-lāk ʿᵊtāh haref yādᵉḵā ("Enough! Now stay your hand") is spoken by YHWH directly to the angel — a command within the divine council that reverses the destruction. The threshing floor of Ornan the Jebusite is identified as the site where the angel stands.
16: wayyiśśāʾ dāwîd ʾet-ʿênāyw wayyarᵉʾ ʾet-malʾak YHWH ʿōmēd bên hāʾāreṣ ûḇên haššāmayim wᵉḥarḇô šᵉlûpâ bᵉyādô — David sees the angel standing between earth and heaven, sword drawn, extended over Jerusalem. The spatial positioning — between spheres — marks the angel as a liminal figure operating at the intersection of divine and human realms. David and the elders respond with full covenant penitence: wayyipᵉlû ʿal-pᵉnêhem (fell on their faces) in śaqqîm (sackcloth) — the full mourning posture.
17: David's second confession explicitly inverts the guilt structure: ʾănî hûʾ ʾăšer ḥāṭāʾtî wᵉhārēaʿ hārēʿōtî — "I am the one who sinned and acted wickedly" — hārēʿōtî is infinitive absolute + perfect for emphatic confirmation of guilt. The contrast hāʾēlleh haṣṣōʾn mah ʿāśû ("these sheep — what have they done?") employs the shepherding metaphor (David as shepherd-king) to argue for redirecting punishment from people to king. The petition ûbi-yādᵉḵā tihi-nāʾ ʾet-yādᵉḵā bî — "let your hand be against me and my father's house" — offers self and dynastic substitution for the people's suffering.
18: The angel's instruction to Gad demonstrates the Chronicler's chain-of-command theology: YHWH → angel → prophet → king; yaʿal dāwîd lᵉhāqîm mizbēaḥ lYHWH ("David is to go up to build an altar to YHWH") transforms the site of the plague's arrest into the site of permanent sacrifice. The threshing floor (gōren ʾornān hayᵉḇûsî) is a liminal space — where harvest grain is separated from chaff — that becomes the dividing line between destruction and mercy, between the old Jebusite past and the Israelite sacred future.
19: wayyaʿal dāwîd biḏḇar-gāḏ ʾăšer dibber bᵉšem YHWH — "David went up in obedience to Gad's word, spoken in YHWH's name." The Chronicler's notation that the prophetic word was "spoken in YHWH's name" (bᵉšem YHWH) is a technical phrase for validated prophecy (cf. Deut 18:20–22). David's immediate obedience after his confession contrasts sharply with the fatal hesitation of the census — repentance here is demonstrated by action, not merely expressed in words.
21: The meeting of David and Ornan has formal protocols: Ornan saw David coming, emerged from the threshing floor (mitten-haggōren), and prostrated himself face-down (wayyištaḥû lᵉdāwîd ʾappayim ʾārᵉṣâ). The double gesture — exit from the floor + full prostration — marks the transfer of sacred space authority from the current occupant to the divinely-designated purchaser. Ornan's role as a Jebusite (a non-Israelite) who held the site that would become the temple mount is a subtle theological statement: YHWH's purposes can be served through any people's land transactions.
23: Ornan's extraordinary generosity — oxen for burnt offering, threshing sledges for wood, wheat for grain offering — offers to supply everything David needs for free. hakōl nātattî ("all of it I give") is emphatic. The verb nātan (give) signals the gift economy that Ornan proposes; David's counter-refusal (lōʾ-ʾeqqaḥ in v24) establishes the purchase economy as the only valid basis for worship. The theological principle is explicit: YHWH's altar cannot be built on someone else's generosity — the worshiper must personally bear the cost.
24: David's foundational statement: lōʾ-ʾeqqaḥ ʾăšer-lᵉḵā lYHWH wᵉhaʿălôt ʿōlôt ḥinnām — "I will not take what is yours for YHWH, or offer burnt offerings that cost me nothing." The word ḥinnām ("for free, at no cost") is the key term; it appears in prophetic indictments of meaningless worship (Mal 1:10) and here establishes that genuine worship requires genuine sacrifice. This principle governs the entire temple-building enterprise: David's preparations (ch22), Solomon's labor, the people's freewill offerings (1 Chr 29) — none of it is costless generosity; all of it is chosen sacrifice.
25: šēš-mēʾôt šeqel zāhāb miśqāl — six hundred shekels of gold by weight. The 2 Sam 24:24 parallel records fifty shekels of silver for the threshing floor and oxen alone; the Chronicles figure reflects purchase of the full temple site (a much larger area) and is thus not contradictory. Gold rather than silver signals the purchase's sacred significance — this is not a normal real-estate transaction but the acquisition of holy ground. The payment establishes legal title that no future Jebusite claim could challenge.
26: The divine response is unambiguous: wayyanswheû bāʾēš min-haššāmayim ʿal mizbah hāʿōlâ — "he answered him with fire from heaven on the altar of burnt offering." Fire from heaven is the sign of divine acceptance in the three great sanctuary inauguration moments: Moses' tabernacle (Lev 9:24), David's altar here, and Solomon's temple (2 Chr 7:1). The Chronicler deliberately echoes all three, constructing a theological chain: one divine fire, three sacred sites, one continuous story of YHWH dwelling with his people. The angel's sword sheathes in the same verse as the fire falls — destruction gives way to acceptance.
27: wayyōʾmer YHWH lammalʾāḵ wayyāšeḇ ḥarbô ʾel-nādānāhh — "the LORD commanded the angel, and he returned his sword to its sheath." The sheathing of the drawn sword (ḥereḇ šᵉlûpâ) that appeared in v16 creates a narrative bracket: plague began with drawn sword / ends with sheathed sword. The altar's dedication simultaneously ends the plague and establishes the permanent cult site — one act accomplishes two things: communal reconciliation with YHWH and the identification of the holy place.
28: The summary statement locates David's sacrifice at the Ornan threshing floor as a direct response to divine answer: kî rāʾâ kî-ʿānāhû YHWH bᵉgōren ʾornān hayᵉḇûsî — "because he saw that YHWH had answered him there." The theology is spatial: YHWH answered at this specific site, marking it as the locus of effective intercession. The threshing floor is no longer Ornan's economic space but YHWH's answer-site. Sacrifice at this location will henceforth be the normative response to YHWH's self-disclosure.
29: The parenthetical note about the tabernacle at Gibeon (ûmiškan YHWH ʾăšer-ʿāśâ mōšeh bammidbār wᵉmizbah hāʿōlâ bāʿēt hahîʾ bāmâ bᵉgiḇʿôn) serves a critical theological function: it explains why David could not simply go to the legitimate Mosaic worship center. The tabernacle and its altar were the authorized cult site, but the angel's presence at Jerusalem made that the effective worship location. The Chronicler is navigating a genuine liturgical tension — the legal center (Gibeon) vs. the providentially designated center (Jerusalem) — that will only be resolved when Solomon builds the temple and brings the ark and tabernacle furnishings together.
30: wᵉlōʾ-yāḵōl dāwîd lāleḵet lᵉḏārōš ʾĕlōhîm kî nibhal mippᵉnê ḥereḇ malʾak YHWH — "David could not go there to seek God because he was terrified before the sword of the angel of YHWH." nāḇal/bāhal (terrified, alarmed) captures genuine theophanic fear, not cowardice. The verse functions as the transition logic: David's inability to use the Mosaic altar at Gibeon is not disobedience but divine redirection. YHWH has effectively relocated authorized worship to the Jerusalem site through the theophany itself — the temple is built where divine fire fell and divine terror prevented departure.
9: waydabber YHWH ʾel-gāḏ ḥōzeh dāwîd — YHWH speaks directly to Gad, whom the Chronicler titles ḥōzeh dāwîd ("David's seer"), using the visionary prophet title (ḥōzeh) rather than the more common nāḇîʾ. The seer tradition (cf. 1 Sam 9:9) emphasizes visual revelation; Gad is the prophet whose role is to mediate YHWH's vision for what David cannot directly see. lēʾmōr ("saying") introduces the divine word that Gad must transmit — YHWH → Gad → David is the complete prophetic chain that governs the chapter's disciplinary structure.
20: wayyiphen ʾornān wayyarᵉʾ ʾet-hammalʾāḵ — Ornan turned and saw the angel; the verb pānâ (turn around) implies he was working with his back to the city when the angel appeared over Jerusalem. wᵉʾarḇaʿat bānāyw ʿimmô yitḥabbᵉʾû — "his four sons who were with him hid themselves" — ḥāḇāʾ (hide, take cover) reflects the instinctive theophanic fear response; ordinary humans conceal themselves before divine manifestations (cf. Exod 3:6, Moses hides his face; 1 Kgs 19:13, Elijah wraps his face). Ornan himself did not hide — he remained at his post, the owner of the site where heaven and earth were intersecting. The contrast between the hiding sons and the standing father sets up Ornan's composed response when David arrives in v21.
